title: Jenkins の 400 エラー：原因と解決策
reasons: Most factual claims are supported by official Jenkins documentation, community discussions, and standard web practices.
The troubleshooting steps provided are practical and align with common Jenkins debugging strategies.
The article's explanation of 400 error causes (malformed JSON, missing parameters, syntax errors) is accurate.
References to the Pipeline Linter and the `/pipeline-model-converter/validate` endpoint are well-grounded in Jenkins' current capabilities.
absolute wording may overstate technical certainty

sources.title: Pipeline Development Tools - Jenkins
sources.claim: Jenkins can validate, or "lint", a Declarative Pipeline from the command line before actually running it. This can be done using a Jenkins CLI command or by making an HTTP POST request with appropriate parameters. We recommended using the SSH interface to run the linter.

sources.title: My Jenkins Story: Validating Jenkinsfile Pipelines | by Colin But - Medium
sources.claim: Jenkins docs give plenty of methods of validating your pipeline code. Check out: https://jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/development/ for more details on all the ways possible. But the easiest way I find is simply making a http call to the running instance of Jenkins server to validate it as follows: curl --user jenkinsuser:password -X POST -F "jenkinsfile=<Jenkinsfile" http://localhost:7000 / pipeline-model-converter/validate.

sources.title: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline [With Examples]: Detailed Guide - TestMu AI
sources.claim: Jenkins declarative pipeline should be the preferred way to create a Jenkins job as they offer a rich set of features, come with less learning curve & no prerequisite to learn a programming language like Groovy just for the sake of writing pipeline code. We can also validate the syntax of the Declarative pipeline code before running the job. It helps to avoid a lot of runtime issues with the build script.

sources.title: How to Use Jenkins Declarative Pipelines - OneUptime
sources.claim: A Declarative Pipeline is a simplified way to write Jenkins Pipeline code. It uses a predefined structure with specific blocks and sections that make your pipeline more readable and maintainable.

sources.title: jenkinsfile-validator | Skills Marke... - LobeHub
sources.claim: Jenkinsfile Validator is a developer-focused skill for validating, linting, testing, and auditing Jenkins pipeline files (Declarative and Scripted) and Shared Library sources. It performs directive and structure checks (pipeline, agent, stages, environment, options, when), Groovy/scripted validations (syntax, node/agent usage, @NonCPS patterns, try-catch-finally), and common audits such as hardcoded credentials, variable scope issues, anti-pattern detection, and controller-heavy operations.

sources.title: Pipeline development utilities - CloudBees Documentation
sources.claim: Jenkins can validate, or "lint", a Declarative Pipeline from the command line before actually running it. This can be done using a Jenkins CLI command or by making an HTTP POST request with appropriate parameters.

sources.title: Validate your Jenkinsfile from within VS Code
sources.claim: jenkins. pipeline. linter. connector. url is the endpoint at which your Jenkins Server expects the POST request, containing your Jenkinsfile which you want to validate. Typically this points to http://<your_jenkins_server:port>/pipeline-model-converter/validate.

sources.title: GitHub - jenkinsci/jenkinsfilelint: Validate Jenkinsfile syntax locally using pre-commit
sources.claim: jenkinsfilelint sends your Jenkinsfiles to your Jenkins server's /pipeline-model-converter/validate endpoint for real syntax validation. It's primarily a pre-commit hook, but also works as a CLI tool.

sources.title: How to validate your Jenkinsfile locally before committing - DEV Community
sources.claim: Connector URL: Enter your Jenkins server URL here as the format below shows: https://<JENKINS SERVER URL>/pipeline-model-converter/validate.

sources.title: GitHub - mschuchard/linter-jenkins: Jenkinsfile lint via declarative linter for pulsar
sources.claim: Linter-Jenkins aims to provide functional and robust declarative-linter linting functionality within Pulsar. This will lint your Jenkinsfile s using declarative syntax.

sources.title: jenkins-notes/jenkins-api.md at master · tfennelly/jenkins-notes - GitHub
sources.claim: Jenkins has a HTTP based API that can be accessed on the Jenkins instance through the /api/ subpath. Docs are available on your Jenkins instance if you can locate the REST API link.

sources.title: Jenkins Pipeline Linter Connector (fork) - Visual Studio Marketplace
sources.claim: url : is the endpoint at which your Jenkins Server expects the POST request, containing your Jenkinsfile which you want to validate. Typically this points to <your_jenkins_server:port>/pipeline-model-converter/validate .

sources.title: jenkins - How can I test a change made to Jenkinsfile locally? - Stack Overflow
sources.claim: To tackle this issue, you can validate the Jenkinsfile against your Jenkins instance (running at $JENKINS_HTTP_URL ): curl -X POST -H $(curl '$JENKINS_HTTP_URL/crumbIssuer/api/xml?xpath=concat(//crumbRequestField,":",//crumb)') -F "jenkinsfile=<Jenkinsfile" $JENKINS_HTTP_URL/pipeline-model-converter/validate.

sources.claim: macOS. Log files should be at /var/log/jenkins/jenkins. log , unless customized in org. jenkins-ci. plist .

sources.title: Getting a JSON Object as a Parameter in a Jenkins Pipeline | Baeldung on Ops
sources.claim: Jenkins provides a readJSON step that we can use to read JSON data from an input parameter or a file. It's a pipeline utility that can parse our JSON text and is the easiest way of working with JSON in our pipeline.

sources.title: Remote Access API - Jenkins
sources.claim: Jenkins provides machine-consumable remote access API to its functionalities. Currently, it provides data in three flavors: XML. JSON with JSONP support. Python-compatible JSON variant.

sources.title: Pipeline Utility Steps - Jenkins
sources.claim: readJSON : Read JSON from a file in the workspace or text. Reads a file in the current working directory or a String as a plain text JSON file. The returned object is a normal Map with String keys or a List of primitives or Map.

sources.title: How make a stage in Jenkins Pipeline that will check a URL for a specific JSON response and succeed/fail? - Stack Overflow
sources.claim: You can use CURL in shell script to call that URL, if you receive anything other than 200 then exit from the script with the error code exit xxx ie. exit 125 .

sources.title: Parsing a REST API bad request HTML-formatted response - Ask a question - Jenkins
sources.claim: Sometimes a bad request is made to the REST API and Jenkins responds with an HTML body describing the error. I wish to handle these responses in my jenkins-rest scripted client rather than dump the HTML back at the user in their console.

sources.title: What is the format of the JSON for a Jenkins REST buildWithParameters to override the default parameters values - Stack Overflow
sources.claim: The JSON data\payload is not sent as the call's json_body (which is what confused me), but rater in the data payload as: json:'{ "parameter": [ {"name":"product", "value":"123"}, {"name":"suites", "value":"high"}, {"name":"markers", "value":"Hello"} ] }'

sources.claim: Note that if you use Stapler ( JSONObject ) for marshalling and unmarshalling JSON, you need an empty constructor.

sources.title: Taming the Jenkins JSON API with Depth and "Tree" - CloudBees
sources.claim: There is, however, a nicer way to control the data back - the "tree" query parameter - here you specify what elements and sub elements of a tree you want returned (nesting as needed) - this simple thing lets you craft a URL that specifies just the data you want back (at the cost of a horrendously long url that takes some time to tune)

sources.claim: jenkins provides the ability to lint declarative pipelines from the command line the linting process will highlight syntax issues for your declarative pipelines before you run them here's today's starting point i have a Jenkins LTS controller version 2.332. 2.

sources.title: Where are the Jenkins System Logs? - YouTube
sources.claim: by default if you want to take a look at the system logs you're going to run journalctlujenkins. or jenkins. service to be more explicit. if you want to customize. where that log. lives then you're going to run systemctlit jenkins and make a change to the environment variable.

unsupported_claims: Jenkinsfilesで外部APIを呼び出す場合、認証情報が正しく設定されているかを確認してください。認証ヘッダーが漏れていたり、トークンが期限切れになっていたりすると、Jenkinsが外部サービスからの応答をパースできず400エラーが返されることがあります。
improvement_suggestions: Optional improvement: add official or primary source URLs for unsupported or weakly sourced claims.

Optional improvement: Clarify the specific causal chain and source of a 400 error when Jenkins fails to parse a response from an external service. It is more common for Jenkins to receive a 400 from the external service due to a malformed request, or for the build to fail with a different internal error if an external response cannot be parsed.
Optional improvement: Review current official docs and update changed API/spec/pricing details.
Optional improvement: Soften absolute claims unless they are directly sourced.
Optional improvement: Add official or primary source URLs for unsupported claims.
